perturbation.original_text: shall be governed by and enforceable under, the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: shall be governed by and enforceable under, the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to California could create inconsistencies, as New York and California have different statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims. This could affect the enforceability of certain provisions depending on the breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 sets a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract. This may contradict the parties' expectations or other provisions in the agreement if they were relying on New York's six-year statute of limitations. A shorter period could limit the parties' ability to bring a breach of contract claim.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an account of o
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from New York to California, which creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 sets a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract, while New York has a six-year statute of limitations. The Perturbation Explanation states that this could affect the enforceability of certain provisions depending on the breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. TITLE 2. CHAPTER 3. 337, confirms that California law mandates a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon any contract. Because the original contract specified New York law, changing it to California law directly contradicts the parties' expectations under the original agreement and impacts their ability to bring a breach of contract claim, making the change an enforceable contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient or its Representatives shall provide the Disclosing Party with prompt notice of any such request or requirement so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient or its Representatives shall provide the Disclosing Party with notice of any such request or requirement within 5 days so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the notification period to 5 days may be insufficient, especially in complex legal proceedings. This timeframe may not allow the disclosing party adequate time to respond, seek legal counsel, or prepare a protective order. This contradicts the broad scope of protection intended in the original agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45 governs subpoenas and allows a reasonable time to comply. Requiring notice within 5 days could be deemed unreasonable in many cases where a subpoena or other legal process demands quick action. This can impede the disclosing party's ability to adequately protect their proprietary information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 5-day notification deadline, potentially conflicting with the 'reasonable time' standard implied by FRCP 45 regarding subpoenas. The Law Explanation points out that a 5-day notice might not be reasonable in all subpoena cases, hindering the disclosing party's ability to protect proprietary information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to Rule 45, outlines the requirements for subpoenas, indirectly supporting the argument that the fixed 5-day period could be deemed unreasonable in certain contexts. The original contract's 'prompt notice' ensured flexibility, which the changed text restricts, potentially creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that until the expiry of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets; provided, however, that such restrictions (or the restrictions set forth in the next sentence) shall terminate in the event that a third party publicly commences a tender or exchange offer, or otherwise proposes a merger or business combination transaction, in respect of the other Party and such other Party has (a) exempted such transaction for purposes of Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law, or (b) publicly announced or publicly confirmed that it is engaged in negotiations in connection with such proposed transaction; provided, further, any such termination shall not relieve the parties of any obligations hereunder with respect to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that until the expiry of six (6) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets; provided, however, that such restrictions (or the restrictions set forth in the next sentence) shall terminate in the event that a third party publicly commences a tender or exchange offer, or otherwise proposes a merger or business combination transaction, in respect of the other Party and such other Party has (a) exempted such transaction for purposes of Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law, or (b) publicly announced or publicly confirmed that it is engaged in negotiations in connection with such proposed transaction; provided, further, any such termination shall not relieve the parties of any obligations hereunder with respect to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the standstill period to 6 months might be problematic. A six-month standstill agreement might be insufficient protection against hostile takeovers under certain state laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law provides certain protections against hostile takeovers. While the parties can contractually agree to restrictions, a six-month standstill may not be adequate to provide the protections intended under Delaware law, potentially conflicting with the spirit and intent of DGCL 203 in a circumstance where the company is incorporated in Delaware.

If an agreement of merger is adopted by a constituent corporation by action of its board of directors and without any vote of stockholders pursuant to this subsection, the secretary or assistant secretary of the constituent corporation shall certify on the agreement that the agreement has been adopted pursuant to this subsection and that the conditions specified in the first sentence of this subse
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Delaware Code Title 8. Corporations § 203 | FindLaw Delaware Code Title 8. Corporations § 203. Business combinations with interested stockholders Current as of January 01, 2022 | Updated by FindLaw Staff (a) Notwithstanding any other provisions of this chapter, a corporation shall not engage in any business combination with any interested stockholder for a period of 3 years following the time that such stockholder became an interested stockholder, unless: (1) Prior to such time the board of directors of the corporation approved either the business combination or the transaction which resulted in the stockholder becoming an interested stockholder; (2) Upon consummation of the transaction which resulted in the stockholder becoming an interested stockholder, the interested stockholder owned at least 85% of the voting stock of the corporation outstanding at the time the transaction commenced, excluding for purposes of determining the voting stock outstanding (but not the outstanding voting stock owned by the interested stockholder) those shares owned (i) by persons who are directors and also officers and (ii) employee stock plans in which employee participants do not have the right to determine confidentially whether shares held subject to the plan will be tendered in a tender or exchange offer; or (3) At or subsequent to such time the business combination is approved by the board of directors and authorized at an annual or special meeting of stockholders, and not by written consent, by the affirmative vote of at least 66 2/3% of the outstanding voting stock which is not owned by the interested stockholder. (b) The restrictions contained in this section shall not apply if: (1) The corporation's original certificate of incorporation contains a provision expressly electing not to be governed by this section; (2) The corporation, by action of its board of directors, adopts an amendment to its bylaws within 90 days of February 2, 1988, expressly electing not to be governed by this section, which amendment shall no
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the standstill period from 18 months to 6 months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that a 6-month standstill might not provide adequate protection against hostile takeovers under Delaware law, potentially conflicting with the intent of DGCL 203. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (Delaware Code Title 8. Corporations § 203) addresses business combinations with interested stockholders and outlines a 3-year restriction under certain circumstances. While the contract doesn't explicitly violate the 3-year restriction, reducing the standstill period to 6 months weakens the protection against hostile takeovers that Delaware law aims to provide, creating a potential contradiction. The original contract, with its 18 month term, afforded significantly more protection, making the modified version non-compliant with the spirit and intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of three (3) years after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of one (1) year after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival period for proprietary information protection to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer useful life and would require more than one year to reverse engineer or otherwise misappropriate. This can violate state trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_citation: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 4548
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no specific duration mentioned for trade secret protection under New York law, the protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret and provides a competitive advantage. Limiting the contractual protection to only one year, even if the information remains a trade secret beyond that, could be seen as a waiver of rights beyond the contractual term and contradict the spirit of protecting trade secrets. The 'law_url1' and 'law_url2' need to be updated. The official source for this law is not available at this time and the second URL does not direct to the law itself. CPLR 4548 is the closest available match. Because this law cannot be verified, this is not the best answer.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.

perturbation.justification: The original contract provides for a three-year survival period for proprietary information protection, whereas the changed contract reduces this period to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states that a one-year period 'may be insufficient to protect trade secrets' potentially violating state trade secret laws. The Law Explanation suggests this could be seen as a waiver of rights beyond the contractual term, contradicting the spirit of protecting trade secrets. While the snippets provided (NYS Open Legislation regarding privileged communications) do not directly address trade secret protection duration, the Perturbation and Law Explanations, taken together, indicate that reducing the protection period may not adequately safeguard proprietary information, especially if its competitive advantage extends beyond one year. This creates a legal contradiction as the modified term could be seen as undermining the inherent protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, unless and until a definitive agreement between each of the Parties with respect to a Business Arrangement has been executed and delivered, neither of us will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever with respect to any such transaction by the Party's Representatives except, in the case of this Agreement, for the matters specifically agreed to herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, unless and until a definitive agreement between each of the Parties with respect to a Business Arrangement has been executed and delivered, neither of us will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever with respect to any such transaction by the Party's Representatives except, in the case of this Agreement, for the matters specifically agreed to herein. This agreement shall not be subject to specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'This agreement shall not be subject to specific performance' could conflict with general principles of contract law where specific performance may be an available remedy, particularly for unique business arrangements and breaches of confidentiality. While parties can limit remedies, outright prohibiting specific performance might be unenforceable or against public policy in some jurisdictions if monetary damages are inadequate.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-716 governs the buyer's right to specific performance in contracts for the sale of goods. While this agreement relates to a potential business arrangement (and thus likely not directly covered by UCC Article 2), the principle of specific performance as a remedy when goods are unique or other circumstances warrant it is a generally accepted principle. Prohibiting specific performance in this agreement could be viewed as an attempt to unduly limit remedies if the potential business arrangement involves unique assets or circumstances making monetary damages inadequate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2)The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The added clause 'This agreement shall not be subject to specific performance' directly contradicts the principle outlined in UCC § 2-716, which states that 'Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances.' While the agreement may not directly involve the sale of goods, the principle of specific performance is generally accepted as a remedy. By explicitly prohibiting it, the modified text attempts to limit remedies in a way that may be unenforceable if monetary damages are inadequate, potentially undermining the legal recourse available to parties.
